    Home › Aircraft › Hughes H-4 Hercules

    Hughes H-4 Hercules

    Hughes · Flying boat · Preserved — flew once, 1947

    The legendary Spruce Goose.

    Length66.65 m
    Wingspan97.54 m
    MTOW180 t
    Built1
    First flight1947
    Height24.18 m
    Our numbers

    Derived metrics

    Computed by aircraft.fyi from the core specification — see how we calculate these.

    Span-to-length ratio#1 of 43
    1.46 wingspan ÷ fuselage length
    Weight per metre#33 of 43
    2.70 t/m MTOW ÷ fuselage length
    Sense of scale

    How big is it, really?

    The Hughes H-4 Hercules's wingspan, against things you already know the size of.

    Hughes H-4 Hercules97.54 m
    Football pitch (width)68 m
    Blue whale25 m
    New Routemaster bus11.2 m
    You (probably)1.75 m
    #12 of 43 by length → #28 by weight → #43 by speed →
    Specification

    The numbers

    Length66.65 m
    Wingspan97.54 m
    Height24.18 m
    MTOW180,000 kg
    Engines8 × P&W R-4360 Wasp Major
    StructureLaminated birch (not spruce)
    Only flight2 November 1947 — ≈26 seconds
    Built1

    Compare it

    Put the Hughes H-4 next to something

    vs Tupolev Tu-114 →vs Bristol Brabazon →
    vs Antonov An-225 vs Airbus A380 Compare with anything →
    ← PreviousEmbraer E-Jet E2 Next →Ilyushin Il-62

    Last verified: 2026-07-14 · Spot an error? business@luck.fyi

    The story

    What makes it different

    Howard Hughes built the largest aircraft in the world out of birch, because wartime rules said he couldn't have aluminium. The H-4 was meant to carry 750 troops or a Sherman tank across the Atlantic, above the U-boats. The war ended before it was finished; the press mocked it as the Spruce Goose, a name Hughes hated for the rest of his life.

    On 2 November 1947, during what was announced as a taxi test in Long Beach harbor, Hughes lifted it off the water himself — about 21 metres up, just over a kilometre, roughly 26 seconds. It never flew again. He kept it in climate-controlled secrecy, maintained by a full crew, until his death nearly thirty years later.

    Its 97.54-metre wingspan remained the widest of any aircraft ever flown until the Stratolaunch carrier aircraft finally passed it in 2019 — seventy-two years later. The complete aircraft is preserved at the Evergreen Aviation Museum in Oregon.

    Variants

    The Hughes H-4 Hercules line

    H-4 Hercules
    The sole aircraft — preserved complete at Evergreen Aviation & Space Museum, McMinnville, Oregon.
    Operators

    Who flies the Hughes H-4 Hercules

    No current operators — this aircraft's flying days are over. The operators of record are below.

    USHughes AircraftNever entered service — one flight, one pilot: Howard Hughes himself.—

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
